explanation: The money question comes out fast and linked: how much rent is three stressed syllables in a row.
answer: How much rent do you pay

explanation: He already hesitates over the rent question, and after "How much do you earn?" he finds an excuse to walk away.
prompt: Why does Guest C stop the conversation?
options: The questions have become too personal
He cannot understand the other guest
answer: The questions have become too personal

explanation: Countable nouns take many, not much. And here, not there: the speaker is at the party.
text: It's great that so many people are here

explanation: Rarely is easy to mishear as really: only the first vowel tells them apart.
answer: But she rarely calls

explanation: "by text message!" comes after a pause and carries the main stress of the sentence.
prompt: How did Tara's ex-boyfriend end the relationship?
answer: By text message

explanation: "Everyone" is singular, so the verb is writes. And you write on a wall, not to it.
text: Everyone just writes Happy Birthday on my wall

explanation: Annie says "Oh, marketing. Boring!" and Rachel immediately answers "Same as Mark. He works in marketing".
prompt: Why is there an awkward pause after Annie speaks?
options: She has just called Mark's job boring
She has turned down an invitation
answer: She has just called Mark's job boring

explanation: "I occasionally watch the rugby on TV, but I'm not a big sports fan" is a qualification, not a flat no.
prompt: How does Leo feel about sport?
options: He occasionally watches rugby on TV
answer: He occasionally watches rugby on TV

explanation: Here down the street means "further along the street", not "downwards".
answer: I'm going to the cafe down the street

explanation: Two clauses in one sentence: the condition if ... at the front and the time clause when ... at the end.
text: if you have good friends you can talk about everything when you meet

explanation: "It's more fun to tell people your news when you can have a real conversation" is her conclusion, not a complaint.
prompt: What is Nina's main argument?
options: News is better told face to face
She is not good at writing letters
answer: News is better told face to face

explanation: "Er, not really" is a short, honest admission; the excuses come afterwards.
prompt: How does Nina answer Chris's first question?
options: She admits she is not good at keeping in touch
She says she writes every day
She does not answer the question
answer: She admits she is not good at keeping in touch

explanation: In fast speech hardly ever runs together as one unit.
answer: I hardly ever phone my parents

explanation: Found out means "discovered". In fast speech the /d/ and the /aʊ/ run together.
answer: And I found out that I love water skiing

explanation: That is the whole point of the week: Richard cannot say no to a question.
prompt: Why did the instructor choose Richard to go first?
options: He asked and Richard said yes
answer: He asked and Richard said yes

explanation: The week is finished, so he uses had been, not "was".
text: The week had been fun but I wanted some control of my life again

explanation: The little word just here means "simply" - it is unstressed and easy to lose.
answer: I just closed the door again

explanation: "all the other passengers were waiting for me. It was a bit embarrassing".
prompt: Why was boarding embarrassing for him?
options: All the other passengers were already waiting
He sat in the wrong seat
answer: All the other passengers were already waiting

explanation: She repeats it three times to herself before she says it out loud: "He lives in Stratford now!"
prompt: What is the problem in Part 2?
options: Her brother has moved to another town
answer: Her brother has moved to another town

explanation: How often asks about frequency. The two words run together as /haʊˈɒfn/.
answer: How often do the trains leave

explanation: "four" and "fourteen" differ only in the ending, and the employee pauses before the number.
prompt: How long until the next train when Annie asks?
answer: Four minutes

explanation: In questions we normally use anything, and the preposition with goes to the end.
text: Is there anything else I can help you with

explanation: "I know, but in Sydney the harbour is such a big part of the city - you'll see." It is a soft disagreement, not a correction.
prompt: Why does Katie disagree slightly when Lucila mentions Lima?
options: In Sydney the harbour is part of the city itself
She thinks Lima has no beaches
She has never been to Lima
answer: In Sydney the harbour is part of the city itself

explanation: "I finish work at six, so we can meet in the city centre."
prompt: Why can Katie meet Lucila in the city centre and not earlier?
answer: She works until six

explanation: After when the verb is present, even though the meaning is future.
answer: I'll send you a text when I get on the train

explanation: The tiredness was going on at that moment, so it is was feeling, not "felt".
text: I completely forgot that I was feeling tired

explanation: The contraction I've almost disappears before "never" - listen for the /v/.
answer: I've never known someone to do that

explanation: It is an ironic comment: she had been given a chair, then ran across the shop.
prompt: Why does Janie say "So much for feeling unwell"?
options: The lady suddenly showed she was fine
The lady asked for a doctor
answer: The lady suddenly showed she was fine

explanation: With never the verb stays in the present perfect: I've never seen.
text: I've never seen crowds like this

explanation: Note the word order: the object can go after the whole phrasal verb, given away my tablet.
answer: I've already given away my tablet

explanation: "Trying to understand which things are really important ... and then saying goodbye to all the rest."
prompt: What does "living with less" mean for Daniel?
options: Keeping only what you really need
answer: Keeping only what you really need

explanation: Annie mentioned the birthday the week before; Mark has forgotten and objects that they hardly know Leo.
prompt: Why does Rachel want to go into the shop?
options: They need a birthday present for Leo
Mark wants to buy himself something
answer: They need a birthday present for Leo

explanation: The other two lines belong to the assistant at the till in Part 3.
prompt: What does the shop assistant ask first?
options: How would you like to pay?
answer: Can I help you?

explanation: Could you ...? is the polite form for a request in a shop.
answer: Could you show us something else

explanation: In questions we use anything, and short adjectives take -er, not "more".
text: Do you have anything cheaper

explanation: "You can go online and pay with your credit card. I've given money to Greenpeace that way a few times recently."
prompt: What does Jack say is the easiest way to give?
options: Paying online with a credit card
Putting cash in a collection box
answer: Paying online with a credit card

explanation: Her own happy childhood is the reason she gives first, before any method.
prompt: Why does Shona support Oxfam in particular?
options: She wants children in poorer countries to have happy childhoods
She has friends who work there
answer: She wants children in poorer countries to have happy childhoods

explanation: The double signal of "never" plus the present perfect makes this a statement about her whole life so far.
answer: I've never given any money to a charity

explanation: Advice to oneself takes should; the purpose clause uses so that ... can.
text: I think I should do something now so that my children can enjoy the world

explanation: Negative going to: the "not" is squeezed between "am" and "going".
answer: I'm not going to leave the house on Saturday

explanation: "This year one of my favourite DJs is playing. I really can't wait."
prompt: Why is Marta especially excited this year?
options: One of her favourite DJs is playing
It is her last year at college
answer: One of her favourite DJs is playing

explanation: "Sleep" is uncountable, so it takes as much ... as.
text: I'm going to get as much sleep as possible

explanation: He even offers to take the day off work and asks "shall I tell the taxi driver to turn round?"
prompt: What does Harry suggest when they are stuck in traffic?
options: Changing the flight and staying another day
answer: Changing the flight and staying another day

explanation: "the palace is nice but it's so crowded. It's really just a place for tourists."
prompt: Why does Harry rule out the Imperial Palace?
options: It is crowded and full of tourists
answer: It is crowded and full of tourists

explanation: The adverb quickly comes after the verb it describes.
answer: You just eat quickly and then you leave

explanation: Mark repeats "since you're a sports fan" - he still has not heard what Leo said a minute earlier.
prompt: Why is Mark's suggestion of a football match awkward?
options: Leo has just said he cannot stand football
answer: Leo has just said he cannot stand football

explanation: "Oh, that sounds nice. I'll just check." The refusal only comes after she looks.
prompt: How does Rachel react to the first invitation?
options: She likes the idea but needs to check
answer: She likes the idea but needs to check

explanation: A polite offer: Would you like + object + to + verb.
answer: Would you like us to bring anything

explanation: The preposition is with, and in this invitation "some time" means "at some point".
text: Do you want to come with me some time

explanation: "you just sit around and talk to people about all the usual stuff until it's time to go home."
prompt: Why does Sven find ordinary parties boring?
options: You just sit and talk about the usual things
answer: You just sit and talk about the usual things

explanation: "What I do like is if we all cook something together, or if everyone makes something and brings it."
prompt: What does Barbara prefer?
answer: Everyone cooking or bringing something

explanation: After look forward to the next word is a noun or an -ing form, never an infinitive.
answer: I'm looking forward to it

explanation: Better is already a comparative, and the person feels bored, not "boring".
text: I think it's better to do something together then you don't get bored

explanation: Two -ing forms in a row - they both depend on "the other thing I love doing".
answer: getting on my skateboard and going around town

explanation: "But I hated my job - too much stress!" The advert then "was like an escape".
prompt: Why did Megan leave her job at the bank?
answer: There was too much stress

explanation: In a positive statement it is something, and the day off is a noun phrase: have a day off.
text: You can't really have a day off because there's always something to do

explanation: Two contracted forms in one line: won't and I'll.
answer: But it won't be easy to find a job I'll enjoy

explanation: "There just aren't enough jobs - you have to take what you can find."
prompt: How does Sara describe the job market?
options: There are too many applicants with degrees
answer: There are not enough jobs

explanation: English prefers I don't think it will to "I think it won't".
text: I don't think it'll take long to find work

explanation: "we can do that tomorrow morning - we'll have time."
prompt: Why does Rachel change her mind about the birthday party order?
options: There will be time in the morning
Tina has too much work already
answer: There will be time in the morning

explanation: "I'll be fine! Don't worry about it." and later "Of course. It's no problem."
prompt: How does Tina respond to Rachel's worries?
options: She insists it is no problem
She offers to call Annie herself
answer: She insists it is no problem

explanation: The phrasal verb splits: put the alarm on.
answer: when you leave you'll need to put the alarm on

explanation: After How about the verb takes -ing, and "cheer up" wraps around the object.
text: How about taking her some flowers That'll cheer her up

explanation: "I've never worked in a cafe. I don't know anything about it." John answers that they give training.
prompt: Why is Penny hesitant about applying?
options: She knows nothing about working in a cafe
The cafe is too far away
answer: She knows nothing about working in a cafe

explanation: "I don't know. I usually work in a supermarket, but I don't like it much ... I might look for a different job this summer."
prompt: What is Penny doing this summer?
options: She is working in a supermarket
She is working in a cafe
answer: She has not decided yet

explanation: A suggestion in the form of a negative question. The preposition is for.
answer: why don't you apply for a job

explanation: The fixed phrase is good at + -ing.
text: It gets quite busy so you need to be good at working really fast

explanation: A negative imperative plus a reason with because.
answer: Don't borrow money from friends because it creates problems

explanation: The reason comes straight after "because" in the same sentence.
prompt: Why should you not borrow money from friends?
answer: It creates problems

explanation: You spend money on something, never "for" it.
text: Only spend money on things you really need

explanation: The subject of the sentence is an -ing phrase: Seeing those sharks.
answer: Seeing those sharks probably saved my life

explanation: "Well, I was really confused. I thought I was going to die."
prompt: What did Caroline think at the very beginning?
options: That she was going to die
That she had lost her way
answer: That she was going to die

explanation: She is describing her own feeling, so the -ed form is the right one.
text: I was happy to be alive but I was also embarrassed

explanation: She calls it "stupid" at first, then admits it: "he still hasn't replied".
prompt: What is the second thing worrying Annie?
options: Leo has not answered her call or text
answer: Leo has not answered her call or text

explanation: "My boss told me not to talk to anyone else. Because other people are going to lose their jobs, too."
prompt: Why does Annie refuse to speak to her colleagues?
options: Her boss told her not to
answer: Her boss told her not to

explanation: Two standard reactions to bad news, one after the other.
answer: Oh how awful I'm really sorry to hear that

explanation: I wouldn't ... is advice, and the subject of the second clause is an -ing phrase.
text: And I wouldn't worry too much changing jobs could be a good thing

explanation: "I don't think I'm the kind of person who can just listen to a language and learn it."
prompt: Why does Bob doubt his wife's advice?
options: He does not think he can learn just by listening
He does not want to go to Poland
answer: He does not think he can learn just by listening

explanation: "when I get home, I spend all my time thinking and worrying."
prompt: What is Chloe's main problem?
options: She cannot stop thinking about work at home
answer: She cannot stop thinking about work at home

explanation: Confused with -ed: he is describing his own state.
answer: I'm just generally confused

explanation: The activity is relaxing; a person would be relaxed.
text: Friends tell me I should do something relaxing after work

explanation: Note the possessive form Selena's and the past tense of "support".
answer: Selena's mother worked hard and always supported her daughter

explanation: Her mother was an actress but "found it hard to get work, so she had very little money".
prompt: Why was Selena's childhood difficult?
options: Her mother had very little money
answer: Her mother had very little money

explanation: The pattern is find it hard to + infinitive.
text: He found it very hard to accept his father's death

explanation: The phrasal verb here does not split: put on weight.
answer: I don't want to put on weight so I walk to work each day

explanation: The reason comes at the very end of the sentence: "to keep in shape".
prompt: Why does one speaker walk to work every day?
answer: To keep in shape

explanation: "I have problems walking upstairs these days - I really need to get fit."
prompt: What problem does the first speaker describe?
answer: Difficulty walking upstairs

explanation: You suffer from a condition - never "suffer with" or "suffer of" here.
text: They all work 10 to 12 hours a day and they all suffer from stress

explanation: "It hurts all the time - when I walk, when I sit down. I've spent the last few days in bed. And I feel exhausted."
prompt: Why is Leo worried?
options: The pain never stops and he feels exhausted
He thinks he needs an operation
answer: The pain never stops and he feels exhausted

explanation: "It's really important, if you spend a lot of time at a desk in an office, to take regular breaks."
prompt: What does the doctor say about Leo's desk job?
answer: He should take regular breaks

explanation: Note the possessive of time: a week's time.
answer: come back again in a week's time if it doesn't improve

explanation: Advice takes shouldn't, and the prediction uses "going to".
text: But you shouldn't stay in bed that's not going to help

explanation: Some friends "got married and had children and their lives sort of went in another direction", and a couple got job offers overseas.
prompt: Why did some of Lucas's friendships end?
options: Their lives went in another direction
answer: Their lives went in another direction

explanation: That is the goal that made him realise he needed to save.
prompt: What did Jeff want to buy?
options: A new car or his own apartment
answer: A new car or his own apartment

explanation: Short and stressed - so unfit carries the whole point of her story.
answer: I was so unfit

explanation: After before the verb stays in the present, even for future meaning.
text: I think Do I need this before I buy something new

explanation: The infinitive of purpose: to improve answers the question "why?".
answer: So he worked really hard to improve his game

explanation: "I thought you meant the country he was a fan of." The host agrees it is a good point.
prompt: Why is the host's question about Robertson's boyhood team confusing?
options: Michael thinks it means the country
answer: Michael thinks it means the country

explanation: The fixed pattern is from ... to ..., and "until" marks the end point.
text: He moved from team to team until he got his dream move to Liverpool

explanation: The pattern the fact that introduces a whole clause as the object.
answer: I just love the fact that she scores so many goals

explanation: "So I became a football fan and a Sam Kerr fan at the same time."
prompt: How did Kelly become a fan?
options: She saw Sam Kerr play in the World Cup
A friend took her to a match
answer: She saw Sam Kerr play in the World Cup

explanation: A long adjective takes more ... than, and a player is exciting, not excited.
text: In my opinion she's more exciting than a lot of male football players

explanation: He asks "Do any other men go?" and then says "It's not really my kind of thing".
prompt: Why does Leo refuse the yoga class?
options: No other men go to it
It is at the wrong time
answer: No other men go to it

explanation: "I just woke up one day and it was hurting. And then every day it got worse."
prompt: How did Leo's back problem start?
options: He woke up one day and it hurt
answer: He woke up one day and it hurt

explanation: Mean to here means "intend to" - a standard part of an apology.
answer: I didn't mean to make you worry

explanation: The doctor's advice still stands now, so the verb is present: says.
text: I think I should do what the doctor says

explanation: The detail comes as an afterthought at the very end of the turn.
prompt: Where does the reader of How I Met Myself usually read?
answer: On the bus to work

explanation: The speaker even explains the word: "a hurricane - a very strong storm".
prompt: What kind of storm is in Eye of the Storm?
answer: A hurricane

explanation: After stop the verb takes -ing.
answer: It's very exciting I can't stop reading it

explanation: The verb look like takes "like" before a noun, never "as".
text: He sees that the man looks exactly like him he's his double

explanation: Two clauses joined by if, with the present tense after "if".
answer: But I'm sure I'll get the marks I need if I work hard

explanation: "I really need to speak to my lecturer. I might fail the year if she doesn't give me more time."
prompt: What does Speaker A need to do?
answer: Speak to her lecturer

explanation: After if the verb stays present, even when the meaning is future.
text: I have to be quick if I don't decide soon I'll miss the deadline

explanation: After stop + object the verb takes -ing.
answer: it becomes a problem when it stops you doing what you want to do

explanation: "Shy people normally want to communicate with other people. They don't want to be on their own."
prompt: What do shy people normally want, according to Dr Lamb?
answer: To communicate with other people

explanation: Comparison takes less than, and "do speak" is emphatic, not a question.
text: We just speak to other people less than in the past so when we do speak it's more difficult

explanation: "Well, I'm a bit busy. Can I call you back?" - the same phrase Mark uses later with Rachel.
prompt: Why does Bernice end the call quickly?
answer: She is busy right now

explanation: "I'm afraid he's not available - he's in a meeting."
prompt: Why can Annie not speak to Mark?
answer: He is in a meeting

explanation: A standard opening check on the phone, and a standard soft refusal.
answer: Is now a good time Well I'm a bit busy

explanation: You tell someone something; "say" would need "to him".
text: Can you just tell him that I called

explanation: "we could go and meet the teachers if we wanted to ... make an appointment and ask about something one-to-one."
prompt: How does Roberta answer the worry about meeting teachers?
options: You can make an appointment and ask one-to-one
You can email them at any time
answer: You can make an appointment and ask one-to-one

explanation: "I wouldn't mind doing my whole degree online" - a mild positive, not enthusiasm.
prompt: What does Roberta say about doing a whole degree online?
options: She would not mind doing it
answer: She would not mind doing it

explanation: Whenever means "at any time that" - one word, not two.
answer: I was free to study whenever I wanted

explanation: In a negative sentence the determiner is any, not "some".
text: Not really you don't need any special skills it's quite easy

explanation: Why pay ...? without "do" is a common way of arguing a point.
answer: So why pay for a book if you can read it for free

explanation: "I like to have the book in my hand - and keep it afterwards."
prompt: What does Speaker 4 prefer?
answer: A real paper book

explanation: A hypothetical question: would in the main clause, could after "if".
text: But why would you pay if you could get it for free

explanation: "You can explain the problem in detail and avoid getting too angry."
prompt: Why does John prefer letters to phone calls?
options: You can explain in detail and avoid getting angry
Companies must answer letters by law
answer: You can explain in detail and avoid getting angry

explanation: You tell someone something - no preposition before "them".
answer: you should always tell them how you felt

explanation: The presenter says it to John: "it's quite important to know how to complain in the UK, since we're top of the list."
prompt: Which country is at the top of the list?
answer: The UK

explanation: After as soon as the verb stays in the present, even about the future.
text: The first thing is that you should be quick complain as soon as the problem happens

explanation: He struggles to explain: "it's a bit ugly. Well, not ugly but it's not very ... adult."
prompt: Why does Leo really want to return the clock?
options: It was a present and he is not a football fan
answer: It was a present and he is not a football fan

explanation: The refusal is softened at both ends: "I'm terribly sorry" and "I'm very sorry".
prompt: How does the assistant refuse Leo politely?
options: I'm terribly sorry, but we can't give you a refund without a receipt
That is not possible at all
You will have to come back tomorrow
answer: I'm terribly sorry, but we can't give you a refund without a receipt

explanation: I'd like to is the polite form for a request in a shop.
answer: I'd like to return this clock please

explanation: After let the verb has no "to": let you exchange.
text: As it was a present I'll let you exchange it for something else

explanation: "I won't use that company again. I'll go to a local shop instead."
prompt: What does Vicki say she will do instead?
answer: Go to a local shop

explanation: Carry on + -ing means "continue doing".
answer: when I asked for assistance they just carried on talking

explanation: And the result of not knowing: "I made too much food and it was embarrassing."
prompt: Why does Rebecca say she needed to know the numbers?
options: So she had enough food and drink
So she could book a room
answer: So she had enough food and drink

explanation: An imaginary situation: If I were ... I'd ...
text: If I were the manager of a company I'd make sure I replied to every customer

explanation: A neat wordplay: fiction becomes fact, which is also the name of the podcast.
answer: So Star Trek science fiction has become science fact

explanation: "where we find out about interesting ideas from books that became reality."
prompt: What is the podcast about?
options: Ideas from books that became reality
answer: Ideas from books that became reality

explanation: After clever at the verb takes -ing.
text: Verne was very clever at imagining machines that would go on to become a reality

explanation: Two past simple verbs in one sentence, joined by when.
answer: When Grismer saw the pictures he was sure the lizards were special

explanation: The motivation is stated directly, just before he gets on the plane.
prompt: Why did Grismer travel to Vietnam?
options: He wanted to be the one who made the discovery
He was invited by the restaurant
answer: He wanted to be the one who made the discovery

explanation: The long action takes was travelling; the short one stays past simple.
text: While he was travelling the restaurant owner cooked the lizards

explanation: "Obviously, I can't promise anything but I think you've a really good chance of getting a job here."
prompt: What does Mark tell Annie about the job?
options: He cannot promise anything but her chances are good
She has definitely got the job
answer: He cannot promise anything but her chances are good

explanation: Annie mishears it as "the fourth office" and the receptionist corrects her: "No, the first."
prompt: Which office is Mark's?
answer: The first on the right

explanation: An indirect question: after "where" the word order stays normal - the Reception is.
answer: Can you tell me where the Reception is

explanation: Indirect question again: where the office is, not "where is the office".
text: Sorry I got lost Could you tell me where the office is again please

explanation: "all the fields we use to grow food for cows could be used for something else - to grow vegetables or plant trees."
prompt: Why does Uta think artificial meat would help the planet?
options: The fields used for cattle feed could be used differently
It would make people eat less
answer: The fields used for cattle feed could be used differently

explanation: "maybe something like a car that runs on air."
prompt: What does Amir think the new engine might run on?
answer: Air

explanation: The repetition hours and hours is a common way of stressing a long time.
answer: You wouldn't need to study for hours and hours to learn a foreign language

explanation: A hypothetical invention takes would be, and the relative clause needs the -s ending.
text: One really useful invention would be artificial meat that's cheap and tastes good

explanation: Reported speech pushes the verb one step back: "I have never heard" becomes she had never heard.
answer: Megan said she had never heard the parrot scream like that before

explanation: "Megan realised something bad had happened" - the noise came first, the realisation second.
prompt: How did Megan know something was wrong?
options: The parrot made a very strange noise
answer: The parrot made a very strange noise

explanation: The story is in the past, and the choking happened before she was found: had got stuck.
text: Some of Hannah's breakfast had got stuck in her throat she couldn't breathe

explanation: "I had no idea, so I just looked at the front of the books and guessed."
prompt: How did Tanya answer her father's question about the stories?
options: She looked at the covers and guessed
She admitted she had not read them
answer: She looked at the covers and guessed

explanation: Reported speech in the past: two past perfect verbs, had tried and had saved.
answer: I told my mum my sister had tried to ride a cow and I had saved her

explanation: "Then I felt bad and helped her back home." The guilt comes later.
prompt: How did Claire feel after her sister fell off?
answer: Bad - she helped her home

explanation: You tell someone something, and the earlier action takes "had drunk".
text: I put the cup near my mouth and told him I had drunk some

explanation: The other two are ways of disagreeing - the second gently, the third quite sharply.
prompt: Which reaction is a strong agreement?
answer: You're absolutely right.

explanation: A soft disagreement followed immediately by the speaker's own view.
answer: I'm not sure about that I think they're all a very similar size

explanation: The subject of the second clause is "that", so the verb is doesn't mean.
text: Maybe Nissan have factories in Korea but that doesn't mean it's a Korean company

explanation: "We tell them they can always come back. And then they're quite happy to go, mostly."
prompt: How does Emilio persuade people to leave?
options: He tells them they can always come back
He tells them the police will make them go
answer: He tells them they can always come back

explanation: The presenter introduces her: "Jennifer Long is reporting for us from one of the worst of the flooded areas."
prompt: Where is Jennifer reporting from on Day 2?
options: One of the worst flooded areas of Houston
answer: One of the worst flooded areas of Houston

explanation: So far means "up to now" and pairs naturally with the present perfect.
answer: They say they've rescued at least a hundred people so far

explanation: The comparison uses as ... as, and "too dangerous to" means it is impossible.
text: Not with the wind as strong as this It's too dangerous to be out in the street
